point_001: The experimental design does not fully eliminate exposure bias: providing a data-based prefix only removes bias up to position L, after which both the D-prefix and M-prefix conditions use the same auto-regressive generation process, meaning both are equally subject to exposure bias during the evaluated portion of the sequence.
point_002: The corpus-BLEU metric used is non-standard and departs significantly from the original BLEU definition (Papineni et al., 2002), which does not allow cross-instance hypothesis-reference matching. The paper misleadingly describes this variant as a 'well-established' metric when it is not commonly used in MT or NLG.
point_003: The automatic evaluation relies on n-gram matches up to trigrams (n≤3), which sets an extremely low bar. Neural language models are already known to generate plausible short n-grams regardless of exposure bias, making this metric insufficient to detect meaningful quality degradation.
point_004: The EB-C metric is introduced without theoretical or empirical justification. There is no established baseline for how large a distributional difference should be expected, and the fact that GANs score poorly on it is uninformative since GANs are known to underperform on standard metrics as well.
point_005: The human evaluation lacks inter-annotator agreement measures, weakening the reliability of its conclusions.
point_006: The human evaluation design is problematic for disproving exposure bias: evaluators exposed to a generated prefix (which may already contain errors) might perceive subsequent errors as consistent with the prefix rather than as evidence of degradation, making it difficult to detect exposure bias even if it exists.
point_007: The paper's strong claim that exposure bias causes minimal performance loss is overstated given the experimental limitations. A more defensible framing would characterize the kinds of effects exposure bias cannot be having (e.g., total degeneration) rather than claiming it is largely absent.
point_008: The experiments only evaluate pure (ancestral) sampling and do not cover other practically important decoding strategies such as greedy search, beam search, temperature sampling, or nucleus sampling, which are more commonly used in applications and are known to exhibit different distributional properties.
point_009: The qualitative analysis at the start of the paper is anecdotal and too limited in scope to be called an 'experiment.' Referring back to it as supporting evidence in later sections is inappropriate.
point_010: The related work section is thin (only two paragraphs) and omits several important references, including an ACL best paper on the same topic (Schmidt, 2019), as well as Tan et al. (2019), Rennie et al. (2016), and work by Zhang et al. (2019) that already includes a section quantifying exposure bias.
point_011: The choice of WikiText-103 as the evaluation corpus is not well justified. Using WebText alongside a pretrained GPT-2 model—as done in related open-ended generation work—would improve comparability with prior literature.
point_012: No gold-standard baseline for corpus-BLEU is provided. Computing the corpus-BLEU of held-out ground-truth continuations against other ground-truth data would contextualize how meaningful the observed differences between D-prefix and M-prefix conditions actually are.
point_013: The paper does not clearly specify the exact generation procedure (e.g., whether top-k or ancestral sampling is used for Transformer-XL), making it difficult to assess whether restricted sampling artificially limits distributional drift and thus understates exposure bias.
point_014: The abstract's claim that prior work has not shown how serious exposure bias is overlooks the fact that many empirical papers have demonstrated task-specific improvements (in MT, image captioning, etc.) from methods designed to reduce exposure bias, which itself constitutes indirect evidence of its impact.
